title: Common cold pattern
content: Common cold symptoms often include sore throat, runny nose, nasal congestion, sneezing, and a mild cough. Symptoms are usually mild and improve gradually with rest, fluids, and time.

title: Flu-like symptoms
content: Flu-like illness can include fever, body aches, fatigue, cough, headache, and sore throat. Educational support should stay general and should not confirm a diagnosis.

title: Mild fever overview
content: A mild fever can happen with common viral illnesses. General monitoring should focus on duration, whether the temperature is rising, and whether new warning signs appear.

title: Cough and congestion
content: Cough, mucus, and congestion often occur with upper respiratory infections. It is important to watch for worsening breathing difficulty, chest pain, or a fever that does not improve.

title: Sore throat and fever pattern
content: Sore throat with fever can happen with viral or other infections. General guidance should focus on hydration, symptom monitoring, and warning signs rather than diagnosis.

title: Runny nose and sneezing
content: Runny nose and sneezing are common with colds and allergies. General information should consider whether symptoms are mild, improving, and free of urgent warning signs.

title: Body aches and fatigue
content: Body aches and fatigue can happen with viral illness, poor sleep, or other conditions. The chatbot should provide only broad educational guidance and recommend medical care if symptoms are severe or worsening.

title: Hydration and rest
content: For mild cold or flu-like symptoms, general self-care includes rest, drinking fluids, warm liquids for throat comfort, and monitoring whether symptoms improve over the next few days.

title: Managing a sore throat
content: General sore throat self-care may include fluids, humidified air, lozenges if appropriate, and avoiding irritants like smoke. Persistent or severe symptoms need professional review.

title: Managing a mild fever
content: General comfort steps for a mild fever may include rest, fluids, light clothing, and monitoring symptoms. Educational guidance should avoid personal dosing advice or diagnosis.

title: Cough comfort measures
content: For a mild cough, general comfort measures can include fluids, warm drinks, humidified air, and watching whether the cough becomes more severe or lasts longer than expected.

title: Nasal congestion self-care
content: General self-care for nasal congestion may include fluids, rest, saline spray, and humidified air. If symptoms worsen or breathing becomes difficult, medical care may be needed.

title: Monitoring mild respiratory symptoms
content: Mild respiratory symptoms can be monitored by watching duration, checking whether breathing becomes harder, and seeking help if symptoms intensify instead of improving.

title: Over-the-counter medication caution
content: General information about over-the-counter products should stress label reading, age limits, ingredient overlap, and asking a pharmacist or clinician when unsure.

title: Ibuprofen and mild fever
content: Ibuprofen is a common over-the-counter pain reliever and fever reducer for many adults. General guidance can say it is a common option for pain or fever relief, while still reminding the user to follow the package label, check warnings, and ask a pharmacist or clinician when unsure.

title: Direct answer for ibuprofen questions
content: When the user asks whether ibuprofen can be taken, a supported answer may say that ibuprofen is commonly used as an over-the-counter pain reliever and fever reducer for many adults, then immediately remind the user to follow the label and check with a pharmacist or clinician if unsure.

title: Acetaminophen and fever relief
content: Acetaminophen is another common over-the-counter option used for pain or fever relief. Educational guidance should focus on careful label reading and avoiding accidental overuse, especially when combination products are also being taken.

title: Cold medicine combinations
content: Combination cold medicines can contain overlapping pain relievers, decongestants, or cough suppressants. People should avoid doubling ingredients unintentionally when using more than one product.

title: Read the active ingredients
content: A safe general medication habit is to check the active ingredient list on each package. This helps reduce the risk of taking two products that contain the same fever or pain medicine.

title: Medication questions are non-diagnostic
content: MediChat can only give general medication safety information. It should not tell the user exactly what they personally should take or confirm that a medication is right for them.

title: When to ask a pharmacist
content: A pharmacist is a useful resource when a person is unsure about age limits, ingredient overlap, interactions, or how to use an over-the-counter fever or cold medicine safely.

title: Urgent respiratory warning signs
content: Shortness of breath, chest pain, bluish lips, confusion, or rapidly worsening symptoms are urgent warning signs and should prompt immediate medical attention.

title: When to contact a clinician
content: A clinician should be contacted when fever is persistent, symptoms are worsening, coughing is severe or prolonged, or the person is worried about dehydration or breathing changes.

title: High fever or worsening fever
content: A fever that is high, persistent, or getting worse deserves medical attention, especially when it is accompanied by trouble breathing, confusion, dehydration, or severe weakness.

title: Trouble swallowing or severe throat symptoms
content: Severe throat pain, trouble swallowing, drooling, or difficulty breathing are warning signs that need professional medical evaluation rather than home management alone.

title: Dehydration warning signs
content: Signs such as dizziness, very dark urine, not drinking enough, or increasing weakness can suggest dehydration and may need clinician assessment, especially if illness is continuing.

title: Project safety scope
content: This chatbot provides general educational medical information only. It does not diagnose, prescribe, or replace licensed professional care.

title: Perfect demo scenario
content: A strong demo question is a common, non-emergency problem such as mild fever, cough, sore throat, or a simple over-the-counter medication safety question. These match the supported scope best.
